abridged.content:
Cardiac output - the flow that actually perfuses organs

Cardiac output (CO) is the volume of blood ejected by the heart each minute. It is the core flow variable behind organ perfusion. Blood pressure matters, but pressure is not the same as flow.

The formula is CO = HR × SV. Heart rate is beats per minute. Stroke volume is blood ejected per beat. A typical resting adult CO is about 4-8 L/min, commonly around 5-6 L/min. Cardiac index corrects for body surface area; a common CTICU minimum is CI ≥2.2 L/min/m².

Stroke volume is determined by preload, afterload, and contractility. Heart rate modifies output, but excessive heart rate shortens diastolic filling and can reduce stroke volume.

In CTICU, cardiac output must be interpreted with MAP, pulse pressure, vasoactive dose, urine output, lactate, SvO₂, echo, and skin perfusion. A normal MAP does not guarantee adequate oxygen delivery.

expanded.content:
Cardiac output - pressure is not enough

Cardiac output is the amount of blood pumped by the heart per minute. It is the flow that carries oxygen, carbon dioxide, glucose, drugs, heat, electrolytes, clotting factors, inflammatory mediators, and waste products. In CTICU, cardiac output matters because organs fail when flow and oxygen delivery are inadequate.

The equation is CO = HR × SV. The simplicity of the equation is deceptive because HR and SV are influenced by rhythm, AV synchrony, preload, afterload, contractility, valve function, ventricular compliance, intrathoracic pressure, temperature, metabolic demand, and vasoactive therapy.

Heart rate

Heart rate increases cardiac output only if stroke volume is maintained. If SV is 70 mL and HR is 70/min, CO is 4.9 L/min. If HR rises to 100/min and SV remains 70 mL, CO becomes 7.0 L/min. This explains why bradycardia can cause low output when SV cannot compensate.

The limitation is diastolic filling time. At excessive rates, especially around 120-150/min in vulnerable post-cardiac surgery patients, diastole becomes too short. Ventricular filling falls, coronary perfusion time falls, myocardial oxygen demand rises, and stroke volume may fall enough that total cardiac output decreases.

Stroke volume

Stroke volume is the volume ejected per beat. It is usually around 60-100 mL/beat in many adults. It is determined by preload, afterload, and contractility. Low SV causes narrow pulse pressure and may produce compensatory tachycardia and vasoconstriction.

Low SV may result from underfilling, bleeding, tamponade, poor contractility, excessive afterload, RV failure with LV underfilling, valve obstruction, valve regurgitation, dyssynchrony, or rapid arrhythmia. The treatment differs completely depending on the cause.

Preload, afterload, and contractility

Preload is filling/stretch before contraction. Fluid helps only if the ventricle is preload responsive and fluid tolerant. Afterload is the load against which the ventricle ejects. Excessive SVR can reduce LV stroke volume; excessive PVR can cause RV failure. Contractility is intrinsic myocardial force generation and may be impaired after bypass, ischaemia, hypothermia, acidosis, or electrolyte derangement.

MAP versus cardiac output

A useful relationship is MAP ≈ CO × SVR + CVP. This explains why MAP can mislead. MAP can be low because CO is low, SVR is low, or both. MAP can be normal because flow is adequate or because SVR is high despite low flow.

PatternMAPCO/CISVRLikely physiology
VasoplegiaLowNormal/highLowPost-CPB vasodilation; pressure problem more than pump problem
Low-output shockLow or supportedLowNormal/highPump, preload, rhythm, afterload, RV, or obstruction issue
Compensated low flowNormalLowHighVasoconstriction preserving MAP while organs receive poor flow
Mixed shockLowLow/normalLow/variableVasoplegia plus myocardial dysfunction, bleeding, or inflammatory shock

Cardiac index

Cardiac index is CO divided by body surface area. It is measured in L/min/m². A common minimum CTICU target is ≥2.2 L/min/m², but adequacy depends on demand. A CI of 2.3 may be acceptable in a sedated normothermic patient with falling lactate, but inadequate in fever, shivering, anaemia, sepsis, or respiratory distress.

Low cardiac output syndrome

Low cardiac output syndrome after cardiac surgery is a pattern. Concerning signs include CI below 2.2 L/min/m², weak arterial upstroke, narrow pulse pressure, cool peripheries, mottling, delayed capillary refill, urine output below 0.5 mL/kg/hr, rising lactate, worsening base excess, falling SvO₂, escalating vasopressors, and altered mentation if awake.

The cause must be split into preload, rhythm, contractility, afterload, obstruction, and demand. Preload problems include bleeding, vasodilation, low stressed volume, tamponade, high PEEP, or RV failure. Rhythm problems include bradycardia, rapid AF, heart block, loss of AV synchrony, and poor pacing settings. Contractility problems include stunning, ischaemia, graft issues, hypothermia, acidosis, and electrolyte abnormalities. Afterload problems include high SVR for the LV or high PVR for the RV. Obstruction includes tamponade, tension pneumothorax, PE, valve obstruction, or graft/outflow complications.

The endpoint is not a perfect monitor number. The endpoint is adequate oxygen delivery with acceptable haemodynamic cost: sufficient MAP, CI, pulse pressure, falling lactate, acceptable SvO₂, adequate urine output, stable rhythm, manageable filling pressures, and no worsening pulmonary oedema, RV congestion, bleeding, graft compromise, or valve stress.

pearls.content:
Clinical Pearls - Cardiac Output

Pearl 1 - MAP is not flow

A normal MAP can hide low cardiac output if SVR is high.

Pearl 2 - Tachycardia helps until filling time collapses

Heart rate increases CO only while stroke volume is preserved.

Pearl 3 - Pulse pressure is often your stroke-volume clue

Narrowing pulse pressure in a fresh cardiac patient is a warning pattern.

Pearl 4 - CI must match demand

A borderline CI may be inadequate if VO₂ is high.

equipment.content:
Equipment Tie-In - Cardiac Output

Arterial lines show pressure and waveform morphology, not CO directly.

PAC can measure CO/CI and calculate SVR/PVR while also showing filling pressures and SvO₂.

Pulse contour monitors estimate SV/CO from the arterial waveform but are limited by damping, arrhythmia, rapid SVR changes, valve disease, and IABP.

Echo shows chamber size, LV/RV function, tamponade, valve lesions, and gross filling.

targets.content:
Targets and Numbers - Cardiac Output

Typical adult CO is about 4-8 L/min. Common CI target is ≥2.2 L/min/m², with many stable patients around 2.5-4.0. Urine output should usually be ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr, lactate <2 mmol/L or falling, and SvO₂ 60-80% or ≥65-70% if the rest of the picture agrees.

tables.heading: Cardiac output core values
rows: Falls with underfilling, poor contractility, high afterload, tamponade, RV failure
Must match body size and metabolic demand
Flow indexed to body surface area
Low with poor perfusion suggests low-output physiology
Low in low flow or venous congestion

quizQuestions.question: What is the central bedside concept in Cardiac Output and Determinants?
options: Interpret the value as part of a haemodynamic pattern, not as an isolated number
Treat every abnormal number with fluid
Ignore the waveform and rely only on MAP
Use SpO₂ as the only marker of perfusion
quizQuestions.explanation: Cardiac Output and Determinants must be interpreted in context. CTICU physiology depends on patterns across pressure, flow, filling, oxygen delivery, demand, waveforms, and clinical response.

quizQuestions.question: A post-cardiac surgery patient deteriorates and the Cardiac Output and Determinants data conflict with the clinical picture. What is the safest action?
options: Validate the measurement and integrate it with rhythm, waveform, lactate, urine output, echo, and perfusion
Assume the single number is correct and treat it alone
Ignore the patient because the monitor is always right
quizQuestions.explanation: The safest CTICU approach is to validate the measurement, check equipment and waveform quality, then interpret it against the whole clinical pattern before acting.

quizQuestions.question: Why is isolated interpretation dangerous in Cardiac Output and Determinants?
options: Because preload, afterload, contractility, rhythm, ventilation, oxygen content, and metabolic demand interact
Because cardiac physiology has no relationship to clinical signs
Because all post-operative patients have identical targets
Because monitors cannot display useful information
quizQuestions.explanation: Isolated interpretation is dangerous because CTICU haemodynamics are interactive. The same value may mean different things depending on ventricular function, vascular tone, rhythm, ventilation, oxygen delivery, and surgical context.
